With those constraints in mind, I'd want to include practical recipes like painting a single dot, drawing continuous strokes in a loop, handling the paint-wait-dry cycle, setting up multiple instances, and tracing programmatic SVG paths.\n\nI should also flag the gotchas\u2014pigments mix subtractively without alpha, brush pressure is captured at pointer-down and won't update mid-stroke, masks stick until explicitly removed, resolution changes reset the canvas, and the active rectangle optimization can behave unexpectedly on small canvases. I'll provide type signatures in a TypeScript-like format since there are 40+ methods to document, and I'm trying to figure out where this fits best in the existing docs structure.\n\nI'm thinking I should create a dedicated \"Agents & automation\" section with its own sidebar entry, include a machine-friendly spec and complete method signatures, add 6-8 practical recipes, document the invariants and gotchas, and maybe follow the AGENTS.md pattern for LLM compatibility.       "new_str": "      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== AGENTS ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"agents\">\n        <h1 id=\"agents\">Agents &amp; automation</h1>\n        <p class=\"docs-lede\">\n          This page is for coding agents and automation tools &mdash;\n          Cursor, Claude Code, ChatGPT, Antigravity, Windsurf,\n          Continue, Cline, Aider, custom scripts, evaluation\n          harnesses &mdash; that want to drive the canvas\n          programmatically. Humans are welcome too. The information\n          here is dense by design.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-quick-spec\">Quick spec</h2>\n        <p>\n          <strong>What this is.</strong> A real-time pigment-and-water\n          simulation, written in vanilla JavaScript with no\n          dependencies. The library implements the Curtis et al.\n          (1997) watercolor model with Kubelka&ndash;Munk compositing,\n          shallow-water flow on a staggered grid (Foster &amp;\n          Metaxas 1996), and edge darkening. Runs CPU-side by default\n          with an optional WebGL2 render path for compositing.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>How to mount.</strong> The library exposes a\n          factory:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const wc = Watercolor.create(targetEl, options?);</pre>\n        <p>\n          Every method on the returned <code>wc</code> handle is\n          stateful and synchronous. There are no callbacks, no\n          Promises (except <code>traceSVG</code> when passed a URL),\n          and no event-loop coordination required from the caller.\n          The simulation runs on a single\n          <code>requestAnimationFrame</code> loop owned by the\n          instance. The host's only job is to call methods when\n          state changes are wanted.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Coordinate system.</strong> All position arguments\n          (<code>paintAt</code>, <code>paintText</code>, cursor\n          positions) are in <em>grid coordinates</em>: integer cells\n          on the simulation grid, not display pixels. Read\n          <code>wc.grid.width</code> /\n          <code>wc.grid.height</code> for current dimensions. Convert\n          display-pixel to grid via <code>wc.toGrid(clientX,\n          clientY)</code>.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Idle behavior.</strong> The sim auto-idles when no\n          pigment is moving (no recent paint events, no animation\n          running, no visualization). When idle, the RAF loop is\n          essentially free &mdash; it ticks at 60Hz but skips\n          <code>simStep</code> and <code>render</code>. Painting via\n          <code>paintAt</code> automatically wakes the sim.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-signatures\">Type signatures</h2>\n        <p>\n          TypeScript-style signatures for every public method.\n          Getter-setter pattern: omit the argument to read; pass to\n          write. All setters return the API handle (chainable) unless\n          otherwise noted.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// === FACTORY ===\nWatercolor.create(targetEl: HTMLElement, options?: CreateOptions): Instance\n\ninterface CreateOptions {\n  canvasScale?: number       // 0.1..1.0, default auto from target width\n  mobile?: boolean           // override matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')\n  continuousFlow?: boolean   // default true\n  pointer?: boolean          // default true; false disables pointer events\n}\n\n// === BRUSH STATE ===\nbrushSize(): number | brushSize(px: number): Instance     // display pixels\npigment(): number | pigment(name: string | idx: number): Instance\npressure(): number | pressure(v: number): Instance        // 0..1, default 0.7\nflow(): number | flow(v: number): Instance                // 0.05..2.0, default 0.4\npaintLoad(): number | paintLoad(v: number): Instance      // 0.1+, default 1.0\nwaterLoad(): number | waterLoad(v: number): Instance      // 0.2+, default 1.0\ncontinuousFlow(): boolean | continuousFlow(v: boolean): Instance\nusePointerPressure(): boolean | usePointerPressure(v: boolean): Instance\n\n// === PAINTING ===\npaintAt(gx: number, gy: number, gridRadius: number,\n        pigment: number | string, strength: number): Instance\npaintText(text: string, opts?: TextOptions): number   // returns dabs painted\ntraceSVG(svgText: string, opts?: SVGTraceOptions): number  // returns points queued\ncancelSVGTrace(): Instance\n\ninterface TextOptions {\n  x?: number; y?: number; fontSize?: number; sampleStep?: number;\n  strength?: number; pigment?: string | number;\n}\ninterface SVGTraceOptions {\n  pigment?: string | number; brushSize?: number;\n  strength?: number; flipY?: boolean;   // flipY default true\n}\n\n// === SCENES ===\nsetAnimation(name: AnimationName, opts?: object): Instance\ngetAnimation(): string\nsetVisualization(name: VisualizationName, opts?: object): Instance\ngetVisualization(): string\nsetBackground(name: BackgroundName): Instance\nisBackgroundRunning(): boolean\n\ntype AnimationName = 'off' | 'ai' | 'rainy' | 'sunny' | 'windy'\n                   | 'thunderstorm' | 'partlyCloudy' | 'snowing'\n                   | 'snowingAdditive' | 'tornado';\ntype VisualizationName = 'off' | 'kaleidoscope' | 'lissajous'\n                       | 'flowField' | 'pulse';\ntype BackgroundName = 'day' | 'dawn' | 'sunset' | 'night' | 'tornado';\n\n// === TOGGLES ===\nedgeDarkening(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\npauseDrying(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nautoDryBackground(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nfadePainting(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\ntransparent(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nwebgl(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nwebglAvailable(): boolean\nwebglDebugTint(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nmobile(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\nmobileDetected(): boolean\nperf(v?: boolean): boolean | Instance\n\n// === CONTINUOUS PARAMS ===\nfadeHalfLife(ms?: number): number | Instance    // 500..30000\nscale(v?: number): number | Instance            // 1..6, REBUILDS sim\npaperWetness(name: string): Instance\npaperColor(r?: number, g?: number, b?: number): object | Instance\n\n// === ACTIONS ===\nrewet(): Instance        // re-saturate paper\ndry(): Instance          // instant-dry; fires 'driedinstantly' event on targetEl\nreset(): Instance        // clear all paint + paper state\nremoveMask(): Instance\nsplash(arg1?, arg2?): Instance\nsplashPresets(): string[]\npaperWetnessPresets(): string[]\npigments(): { index: number, name: string }[]\n\n// === RENDER + EXPORT ===\nexportPNG(opts?: { mimeType?: string }): string  // returns data URL\nbuildPigmentSwatches(rootEl: HTMLElement): Instance\n\n// === STATE ===\nstate(): StateSnapshot\ngrid: { width: number, height: number }\ntoGrid(clientX: number, clientY: number): { gx: number, gy: number }\nperfMetrics(): PerfSnapshot\ndestroy(): void   // cancels RAF, removes listeners, drops references</pre>\n\n        <p>\n          <strong>Pigment name resolution.</strong> All methods\n          accepting a pigment accept one of:\n          <code>'rose'</code> | <code>0</code>,\n          <code>'yellow'</code> | <code>1</code>,\n          <code>'blue'</code> | <code>'cerulean'</code> | <code>2</code>,\n          <code>'water'</code> | <code>-1</code>,\n          <code>'lift'</code> | <code>-2</code>,\n          <code>'rainbow'</code> | <code>-3</code>,\n          <code>'mask'</code> | <code>-4</code>.\n          Unknown names throw <code>Error: Unknown pigment: &lt;name&gt;</code>.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-recipes\">Recipes</h2>\n        <p>\n          Common tasks as standalone snippets. All assume\n          <code>wc = Watercolor.create(targetEl)</code> has been\n          called and the instance is in its default state.\n        </p>\n\n        <h3>Single dab at canvas center</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const { width, height } = wc.grid;\nwc.paintAt(width / 2, height / 2, 20, 'blue', 0.7);</pre>\n\n        <h3>Continuous stroke from A to B</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Interpolated stamps along the line. Spacing should be roughly\n// brush-radius / 4 for smoothness. paintAt internally takes a\n// grid-radius (not diameter), so divide by 2.\nfunction stroke(x1, y1, x2, y2, brushPx = 20, pigment = 'rose') {\n  const r = brushPx / 2;\n  const dx = x2 - x1, dy = y2 - y1;\n  const dist = Math.sqrt(dx * dx + dy * dy);\n  const steps = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(dist / (r * 0.4)));\n  for (let i = 0; i &lt;= steps; i++) {\n    const t = i / steps;\n    wc.paintAt(x1 + dx * t, y1 + dy * t, r, pigment, 0.7);\n  }\n}\nstroke(100, 100, 500, 400);</pre>\n\n        <h3>Loop-driven painting (timed sequence)</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Paint a sequence with realistic timing: paint, let it bleed for\n// 2 seconds, paint again, let it dry. Note that you do NOT need to\n// step the sim manually \u2014 the lib's RAF runs continuously. You\n// just decide when to inject paint events.\nasync function sequence() {\n  const sleep = ms =&gt; new Promise(r =&gt; setTimeout(r, ms));\n  wc.pigment('rose'); wc.brushSize(40);\n  wc.paintAt(300, 300, 20, 'rose', 0.7);\n  await sleep(2000);                          // bleed for 2s\n  wc.pigment('blue');\n  wc.paintAt(400, 350, 18, 'blue', 0.7);     // wet-on-wet mixing\n  await sleep(3000);                          // bleed more\n  wc.dry();                                   // instant dry\n  const png = wc.exportPNG();\n  return png;\n}</pre>\n\n        <h3>Multi-instance setup</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Each create() returns an independent simulation. No globals\n// are shared between instances; safe to mount many on one page.\nconst instA = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('canvas-a'));\nconst instB = Watercolor.create(document.getElementById('canvas-b'),\n  { canvasScale: 0.5 });   // half-resolution for the smaller embed\ninstA.setBackground('sunset');\ninstB.setAnimation('rainy');</pre>\n\n        <h3>SVG trace with custom brush</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">const svgText = await fetch('/some-vector.svg').then(r =&gt; r.text());\nwc.traceSVG(svgText, {\n  pigment: 'cerulean',\n  brushSize: 30,\n  strength: 0.6,\n  flipY: true   // default; set false for standard Y-down SVGs\n});</pre>\n\n        <h3>Set scene + animation, then capture frames</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Useful for procedurally generating training data / examples.\nwc.setBackground('dawn');\nwc.setAnimation('rainy');\nconst frames = [];\nconst intervalId = setInterval(() =&gt; {\n  frames.push(wc.exportPNG());\n  if (frames.length &gt;= 60) {\n    clearInterval(intervalId);\n    wc.setAnimation('off');\n  }\n}, 1000 / 30);   // 30 fps capture</pre>\n\n        <h3>Listen for sim state changes</h3>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// The library dispatches CustomEvents on targetEl for some\n// state changes. Currently:\n//   'rescaled'         \u2014 fired after scale() rebuilds the sim\n//   'driedinstantly'   \u2014 fired after dry() completes\n//   'pigmentchange'    \u2014 fired by lib-built swatches when clicked\n//                        (only when using buildPigmentSwatches)\ntargetEl.addEventListener('rescaled', (e) =&gt; {\n  console.log('grid is now', e.detail.gridWidth, 'x', e.detail.gridHeight);\n});</pre>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-invariants\">Gotchas &amp; invariants</h2>\n        <p>\n          Surprising-but-true facts. These are things that will save\n          you from chasing phantom bugs.\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt>Coordinates are grid units, NOT pixels.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            <code>paintAt(100, 100, 20, 'blue', 0.7)</code> paints at\n            grid cell (100, 100) with a grid-radius of 20 cells. With\n            the default <code>SCALE = 1.75</code>, those grid units\n            map to about 175 display pixels from the top-left. Use\n            <code>wc.grid.width</code> / <code>wc.grid.height</code>\n            for proportional placement; use <code>wc.toGrid()</code>\n            to convert clientX/Y from mouse events.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>paintAt takes grid-RADIUS, not diameter.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            <code>brushSize()</code> is in display pixels and is\n            diameter. When converting between them: <code>r =\n            (brushSize / 2) / SCALE</code>. The recipe above does this\n            inline.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>Pigments mix subtractively, not RGB-additively.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Red + green = brown-ish (not yellow), red + blue =\n            purple, etc. Driven by Kubelka&ndash;Munk K/S triplets\n            from Figure 5 of Curtis et al. 1997. <code>'water'</code>\n            doesn't deposit pigment; it deposits wetness and dilutes\n            whatever is there. <code>'lift'</code> draws pigment back\n            into suspension.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>scale() rebuilds the sim and clears the canvas.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Changing resolution reallocates all state arrays. The\n            instance dispatches a <code>'rescaled'</code> event on\n            <code>targetEl</code> when done. Don't call\n            <code>scale()</code> in a tight loop &mdash; debounce by\n            ~80ms.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>mask is sticky.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Mask deposits frozen-pigment-protection cells. Painting\n            with <code>'mask'</code> over an area makes that area\n            immutable to subsequent painting + diffusion until you\n            call <code>removeMask()</code>. <code>reset()</code> also\n            clears the mask along with everything else.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>WebGL toggle does not change physics.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            <code>wc.webgl(true)</code> moves the per-pixel\n            Kubelka&ndash;Munk compositing onto the GPU. The bleeding,\n            advection, evaporation, edge darkening &mdash; the actual\n            simulation &mdash; still runs on the CPU. So toggling\n            WebGL changes frame rate, not the resulting painting.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>fadePainting() doesn't fade unpainted pixels.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Only cells with deposited pigment fade. The paper texture\n            itself doesn't decay. If a canvas is fully covered, fade\n            will gradually return everything to paper-tone; if it's\n            partially covered, only the painted regions thin out.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>destroy() is required for clean unmount.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Without it, the RAF loop keeps running and the canvas\n            element keeps capturing pointer events. In test harnesses\n            or hot-reload scenarios, always <code>wc.destroy()</code>\n            before unmounting <code>targetEl</code>.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>SVG trace flipY default is true.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Most SVG sources Y-down per spec, but many CAD/scientific\n            sources are Y-up. The default <code>flipY: true</code>\n            covers the common observed case. Pass\n            <code>{ flipY: false }</code> for standard Y-down sources.\n          </dd>\n\n          <dt>pressure() is captured at pointer-down.</dt>\n          <dd>\n            Changing <code>pressure()</code> mid-stroke does NOT\n            affect the in-progress drag. The strength for the rest\n            of the stroke is locked at pointer-down time. This is\n            intentional &mdash; per-event pressure variability looks\n            chaotic to most users.\n          </dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-loop\">Driving from a loop</h2>\n        <p>\n          Patterns for autonomous canvas driving. The library is\n          designed to be controlled imperatively: you don't queue\n          events, you just call methods when you want changes.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\">// Pattern 1: Step-by-step deterministic sequence\nasync function paint() {\n  for (const step of plan) {\n    if (step.type === 'paint') {\n      wc.paintAt(step.x, step.y, step.r, step.pigment, step.strength);\n    } else if (step.type === 'wait') {\n      await new Promise(r =&gt; setTimeout(r, step.ms));\n    } else if (step.type === 'dry') {\n      wc.dry();\n    }\n  }\n}\n\n// Pattern 2: Continuous control loop with state polling\nfunction controller(targetX, targetY) {\n  let active = true;\n  function tick() {\n    if (!active) return;\n    const s = wc.state();\n    // Decide next action based on current state. For example, paint\n    // while the canvas is \"dry enough\" to absorb new paint cleanly.\n    if (!s.dryingPaused) {\n      wc.paintAt(targetX, targetY, 15, 'rose', 0.5);\n    }\n    requestAnimationFrame(tick);\n  }\n  tick();\n  return () =&gt; { active = false; };\n}\n\n// Pattern 3: Test harness \u2014 discrete steps with deterministic state\nasync function runEval(scenario) {\n  const host = document.createElement('div');\n  document.body.appendChild(host);\n  const wc = Watercolor.create(host, { pointer: false });   // no UI input\n  for (const action of scenario.actions) action(wc);\n  await new Promise(r =&gt; setTimeout(r, scenario.settleMs ?? 1500));\n  const png = wc.exportPNG();\n  wc.destroy();\n  document.body.removeChild(host);\n  return png;\n}</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Determinism caveat.</strong> The sim isn't\n          bit-deterministic across runs &mdash; floating-point\n          ordering, RAF cadence, and the active-rect optimization\n          mean the same input sequence may produce slightly different\n          output. For deterministic evals, render frames yourself by\n          calling internal step methods directly via a fork, or\n          compare results structurally (pigment count, bounding box)\n          rather than byte-for-byte.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>Pointer-free instances.</strong> Pass\n          <code>{ pointer: false }</code> in create options to disable\n          the built-in pointer event handlers. Useful for test\n          harnesses where mouse-driven painting would interfere with\n          programmatic painting via <code>paintAt</code>.\n        </p>\n\n        <h2 id=\"agents-llms-txt\">llms.txt &amp; AGENTS.md</h2>\n        <p>\n          Two emerging conventions for making projects readable by\n          coding agents. Neither is required to use this library, but\n          both work well alongside it if you're embedding the library\n          in a larger codebase.\n        </p>\n        <p>\n          <strong>llms.txt</strong> &mdash; A short Markdown file at\n          your site root that summarizes the project for an LLM\n          fetching it. Recommended location: <code>/llms.txt</code>.\n          A reasonable template for projects using this library:\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"># My Watercolor App\n\nA &lt;one-sentence project description&gt;.\n\n## What it uses\n- Watercolor v0.29 library \u2014 a physical watercolor simulation\n  implementing Curtis et al. 1997. State is held inside a factory\n  instance returned by `Watercolor.create(targetEl, options)`.\n\n## API surface this app uses\n- wc.setBackground(name)\n- wc.paintAt(gx, gy, r, pigment, strength)\n- wc.exportPNG()\n\n## Where to look\n- src/main.js \u2014 instance creation + UI wiring\n- src/scenes/ \u2014 declarative scene definitions\n- public/index.html \u2014 markup the library mounts into\n\nSee the Watercolor library's own `/docs#agents` page for full\nAPI surface and gotchas.</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>AGENTS.md</strong> &mdash; A README-style file\n          aimed at coding agents. Whereas <code>README.md</code>\n          targets humans skim-reading on GitHub, <code>AGENTS.md</code>\n          targets an LLM looking for actionable knowledge. Keep it\n          terse, link to specific files, list constraints\n          explicitly.\n        </p>\n        <pre class=\"docs-code\"># AGENTS.md\n\n## Project shape\n- Single-page web app, no framework, vanilla JS.\n- Entry: `src/main.js` (creates one Watercolor instance).\n- No build step; deploy by uploading `index.html` + `src/`.\n\n## Conventions\n- Async: prefer `async/await`. No callbacks.\n- Naming: camelCase functions, PascalCase classes, ALL_CAPS constants.\n- File length cap: ~500 lines per file; split otherwise.\n\n## When making changes\n- DO NOT add npm dependencies without asking. This repo is\n  zero-dependency by policy.\n- DO NOT modify `vendor/watercolor-lib.js` \u2014 it's an external\n  library. To customize behavior, configure via options or wrap\n  the instance.\n- Tests live in `test/` and run via `node --test`.\n\n## Common tasks\n- Add a new scene: create `src/scenes/yourName.js` exporting a\n  default function `(wc) =&gt; void`, then register in `scenes.js`.\n- Change pigment palette: edit `PIGMENTS` array in\n  `vendor/watercolor-lib.js` \u2014 but consult `docs#agents` for the\n  K/S/density/granularity tuple meanings first.</pre>\n        <p>\n          <strong>For evaluations and benchmarks.</strong> If you're\n          building eval harnesses that drive this library, the\n          test pattern in\n          <a href=\"#agents-loop\">\"Driving from a loop\"</a> above\n          (Pattern 3) is the recommended shape: pointer-free\n          instance, sequence of programmatic paint calls, settle\n          period, capture frame, destroy.\n        </p>\n      </section>\n\n      <!-- ============================== CONCEPTS ============================== -->\n      <section class=\"docs-page\" data-section=\"concepts\">",

    "text": "**Added a full \"Agents & automation\" section to the docs.** Six subsections:\n\n**Quick spec** \u2014 A dense one-paragraph orientation for an agent landing cold. Explains what the library is, how to mount it, the coordinate system, and the idle behavior. The most important thing for an agent is knowing that coordinates are grid units (not pixels) and that the sim runs its own RAF loop so they don't need to step it manually.\n\n**Type signatures** \u2014 Every public method in TypeScript-style signatures. Includes the factory + options interface, all the brush state methods, painting/scenes/toggles/actions, plus pigment name resolution (`'rose' | 0`, `'cerulean' | 'blue' | 2`, etc.) and the exact error string thrown on bad names. This is the kind of thing an agent can pattern-match against without parsing the whole library.\n\n**Recipes** \u2014 Six worked code snippets: single dab, interpolated stroke, async paint-wait-paint-dry sequence, multi-instance, SVG trace with custom brush, frame capture for procedurally generating data, and listening for the lib's CustomEvents (`'rescaled'`, `'driedinstantly'`, `'pigmentchange'`).\n\n**Gotchas & invariants** \u2014 Nine non-obvious things that will save an agent from chasing phantom bugs:\n- Coordinates are grid units, not pixels\n- paintAt takes radius, not diameter\n- Pigments mix subtractively (Kubelka-Munk), not RGB-additively\n- `scale()` rebuilds the sim and clears the canvas\n- Mask is sticky until `removeMask()` or `reset()`\n- WebGL toggle changes frame rate, not physics\n- `fadePainting()` only fades painted pixels, not paper\n- `destroy()` is required for clean unmount\n- SVG `flipY` defaults to true\n- `pressure()` is captured at pointer-down and locked for the stroke\n\n**Driving from a loop** \u2014 Three concrete patterns for autonomous canvas driving: step-by-step deterministic sequence, continuous control loop with state polling, and a test-harness pattern using `{ pointer: false }` to prevent UI input from contaminating programmatic painting. Also calls out the determinism caveat (the sim isn't bit-deterministic across runs, so evals should compare structurally).\n\n**llms.txt & AGENTS.md** \u2014 Templates for the two emerging agent-readable file conventions, tuned to projects embedding this library. `llms.txt` is a project root summary fetched by an LLM with a URL; `AGENTS.md` is a README-style file aimed at coding agents working inside your repo. Both templates include do/don't constraints and specific file paths.\n\n**Sidebar nav** \u2014 New collapsible \"Agents & automation\" group between \"Programmatic API\" and \"Concepts\", with six jump-links to the subsections.\n\n**Changelog v0.29 entry** updated with a new bullet documenting the Agents section and the `{ pointer: false }` create option (which already existed but is now documented).\n\n**Verified:** both inline scripts (lib ~213KB, wiring ~21KB) parse cleanly after rebuild, total HTML is 377KB. The CSS uses two separate attribute selectors \u2014 `aria-expanded` on the button and `data-expanded` on the parent group \u2014 but nothing was toggling either at runtime. So:\n\n- Section headers were unclickable (no click handlers attached)\n- Only the \"Introduction\" group had `aria-expanded=\"true\"` in the static HTML, but its parent group had no `data-expanded=\"true\"`, so even those child links were hidden\n- No scroll spy and no active-link highlighting\n\nRestored the three pieces from v0.26.2:\n\n1. **Accordion** \u2014 clicking a section button toggles `data-expanded` on its group + `aria-expanded` on the button (kept in sync). Open state is independent \u2014 clicking one group doesn't close others, but the scroll spy will.\n\n2. **Scroll spy** \u2014 `IntersectionObserver` on each `.docs-page[data-section]`. As you scroll between sections, the one with the largest intersection ratio becomes \"active\" and its nav group auto-expands while others collapse. `rootMargin: '-30% 0px -50% 0px'` keeps the active zone in the middle of the viewport so a section becomes active when it's mid-screen, not the instant a pixel enters.\n\n3. **Active link** \u2014 a second `IntersectionObserver` watches every `h1[id]/h2[id]/h3[id]` in `.docs-main`. The heading crossing a \"read line\" 90px below the viewport top gets `.active` on its matching nav link. Now the bold accent in the nav follows your reading position.\n\nEdge case I noticed and handled: if the HTML markup is later changed and no group has `aria-expanded=\"true\"`, the JS now opens the first group as a fallback so the user always sees content.\n\n**The \"transparent on mobile\" issue is also fixed.** On screens \u2264 760px, the docs nav was set to `position: static` with no background \u2014 it became a full-height block of links at the top of the docs section that the user had to scroll through before reaching reading content, and there was no visual boundary between nav and body. Updated the mobile rule:\n\n```css\n@media (max-width: 760px) {\n  .docs-nav {\n    position: static;\n    max-height: 40vh;         /* cap, don't eat the screen */\n    overflow-y: auto;         /* scroll internally instead */\n    background: var(--panel-bg);     /* visible surface */\n    border: 1px solid var(--panel-border);\n    border-radius: 6px;\n    padding: 14px 16px;\n    backdrop-filter: blur(10px) saturate(120%);\n  }\n}\n```\n\nReads as a proper distinct widget \u2014 same panel treatment as the controls panel and perf overlay. Capped at 40% viewport height so it can't dominate the screen even when fully expanded. Internal scroll lets you traverse the full TOC without affecting the page scroll.\n\nVerified both scripts parse cleanly after rebuild (lib ~213KB, wiring ~24KB \u2014 up from 21KB due to the nav restoration).",

    "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": " The `splash()` function is the right approach here \u2014 it suspends the deposited pigment back into the medium and imparts radial velocity from the impact point, creating that wet swirling effect that diffuses outward. For the 500ms timeline, I need `splash()` to set up the initial wet state, then let the simulation run for the full duration to spread the pigment across the canvas.\n\nFor the sketch preset, I'm thinking of a single `sketchMode()` call that configures multiple parameters at once \u2014 fine brush size, denser pigment load, minimal water, firm pressure, no continuous flow, bone-dry paper, and disabled edge darkening and fading to get clean, settled lines.\n\nThe `obliterate()` function could either use a strong splash variant or paint water directly across the canvas to destroy the drawing, though I'm realizing the user might want the choice between water or pigment for the obliteration effect.\n\nSo I'm designing `obliterate(opts)` to accept a mode flag \u2014 either 'water' to trigger a splash that lifts pigment, or 'pigment' to paint an opaque wash over the sketch \u2014 along with duration, color, and position parameters. The water mode just calls the existing splash function, while pigment mode paints several large dabs with high paint load to cover and bleed into the sketch.\n\nI'm also adding a `sketch(opts)` preset method that bundles lightweight settings for fine-line drawing \u2014 small brush, low water, high paint, dry paper, and no edge effects \u2014 so users can quickly switch to sketch mode without manually configuring each parameter. I'm settling on a simpler approach \u2014 just add a `sketchMode(opts?)` method that applies the bundle of sketch settings in one call, and users can override individual settings afterward if needed. Then I'll document the full pattern in a new recipe showing how to combine this with the obliteration animation for the project thumbnail workflow.",

      "description": "Add sketchMode() and obliterate() to API",
      "new_str": "  paperWetness(preset) {\n    if (preset === undefined) return null;   // no getter for now\n    if (!applyEvapPreset(preset)) {\n      throw new Error('Unknown paper-wetness preset: ' + preset +\n        '. Valid: ' + Object.keys(EVAP_PRESETS).join(', '));\n    }\n    return api;\n  },\n  paperWetnessPresets() { return Object.keys(EVAP_PRESETS); },\n\n  // v0.30 \u2014 Sketch mode. Bundles a set of brush + paper settings tuned\n  // for crisp fine-line drawings (felt-tip pen aesthetic). Intended for\n  // SVG-traced thumbnails and other \"drawing\"-style uses where bleed is\n  // unwanted. Pure setter \u2014 no separate mode flag, no state to restore;\n  // it just calls the underlying API methods. Caller can override any\n  // individual setting after by calling the corresponding method.\n  //\n  // Applied settings:\n  //   brushSize(opts.brushSize ?? 7)        \u2014 fine tip\n  //   paintLoad(opts.paintLoad ?? 1.6)      \u2014 denser pigment per dab\n  //   waterLoad(opts.waterLoad ?? 0.25)     \u2014 minimal moisture\n  //   pressure(opts.pressure ?? 0.95)       \u2014 firm strokes\n  //   paperWetness(opts.paperWetness ?? 'boneDry')  \u2014 no bleed\n  //   continuousFlow(false)                 \u2014 pure stamps, no hold-drip\n  //   edgeDarkening(false)                  \u2014 clean lines, no rim\n  //   fadePainting(false)                   \u2014 strokes persist\n  //\n  // Returns the instance for chaining: wc.sketchMode().traceSVG(svg).\n  sketchMode(opts) {\n    opts = opts || {};\n    api.brushSize(opts.brushSize !== undefined ? opts.brushSize : 7);\n    api.paintLoad(opts.paintLoad !== undefined ? opts.paintLoad : 1.6);\n    api.waterLoad(opts.waterLoad !== undefined ? opts.waterLoad : 0.25);\n    api.pressure(opts.pressure !== undefined ? opts.pressure : 0.95);\n    api.paperWetness(opts.paperWetness || 'boneDry');\n    api.continuousFlow(false);\n    api.edgeDarkening(false);\n    api.fadePainting(false);\n    return api;\n  },\n\n  // v0.30 \u2014 Obliterate. Visually destroys the current drawing via a\n  // wet wash. Two modes:\n  //\n  //   mode: 'water'   \u2014 splash water onto the canvas, lifting all\n  //                     deposited pigment back into suspension. The\n  //                     pigment swirls outward as it diffuses. Best\n  //                     for \"rinse\" or \"wash away\" feel.\n  //\n  //   mode: 'pigment' \u2014 paint several large opaque dabs over the\n  //                     sketch in the configured color. Best for\n  //                     \"cover up\" feel \u2014 sketch disappears under a\n  //                     wash of color.\n  //\n  // Returns a Promise that resolves after durationMs (default 500).\n  // Intended use: trigger this when a user clicks the thumbnail, await\n  // it, then navigate to the project. Concurrency-safe: multiple\n  // obliterate() calls on the same instance interleave correctly via\n  // the underlying lib state.\n  //\n  // opts: {\n  //   mode: 'water' | 'pigment'  (default 'water')\n  //   pigment: name | index      (default current pigment, for pigment mode)\n  //   durationMs: number         (default 500)\n  //   x, y: grid coords          (default canvas center)\n  //   radiusFraction: number     (0..1, default 0.7 \u2014 fraction of canvas)\n  //   intensity: 'gentle' | 'normal' | 'extreme'  (default 'normal')\n  // }\n  obliterate(opts) {\n    opts = opts || {};\n    const mode = opts.mode || 'water';\n    const duration = opts.durationMs !== undefined ? +opts.durationMs : 500;\n    const cx = opts.x !== undefined ? +opts.x : GW * 0.5;\n    const cy = opts.y !== undefined ? +opts.y : GH * 0.5;\n    const radiusFraction = opts.radiusFraction !== undefined ? +opts.radiusFraction : 0.7;\n    const intensity = opts.intensity || 'normal';\n\n    if (mode === 'water') {\n      // Map intensity \u2192 splash preset. Water mode lifts existing\n      // pigment; the splash() function does exactly this.\n      const presetMap = {\n        gentle:  'fineSpritz',\n        normal:  'default',\n        extreme: 'bigSplash',\n      };\n      const presetName = presetMap[intensity] || 'default';\n      // Use the caller's epicenter so the wash visually originates\n      // from a specific point (e.g. where the user clicked).\n      splash([{ x: cx, y: cy }], presetName);\n    } else if (mode === 'pigment') {\n      // Pigment mode \u2014 paint several large opaque dabs to cover the\n      // sketch. Use the active pigment unless overridden.\n      const pig = opts.pigment !== undefined\n        ? _resolvePigmentOption(opts.pigment)\n        : currentPigment;\n      // Splash some water first so pigment bleeds outward\n      splash([{ x: cx, y: cy }], 'default');\n      // Then drop a few large dabs at + around the epicenter. The\n      // radius is in grid cells; cover most of the canvas at the\n      // chosen radiusFraction.\n      const r = Math.min(GW, GH) * radiusFraction * 0.5;\n      const intensityMul = intensity === 'gentle' ? 0.6\n                        : intensity === 'extreme' ? 1.4 : 1.0;\n      const dabStrength = Math.min(1.0, 0.9 * intensityMul);\n      paintAt(cx, cy, r, pig, dabStrength);\n      // Offset dabs to ensure the whole area gets covered if the\n      // sketch extends past center.\n      paintAt(cx + r * 0.5, cy, r * 0.7, pig, dabStrength * 0.7);\n      paintAt(cx - r * 0.5, cy, r * 0.7, pig, dabStrength * 0.7);\n      paintAt(cx, cy + r * 0.5, r * 0.7, pig, dabStrength * 0.7);\n      paintAt(cx, cy - r * 0.5, r * 0.7, pig, dabStrength * 0.7);\n    } else {\n      throw new Error('Watercolor.obliterate: mode must be \\'water\\' or \\'pigment\\', got: ' + mode);\n    }\n\n    // Wake the sim and let it run for `duration`. The sim is already\n    // running in its RAF loop, so we just wait.\n    markCanvasActive();\n    return new Promise((resolve) => setTimeout(resolve, duration));\n  },",
      "old_str": "  paperWetness(preset) {\n    if (preset === undefined) return null;   // no getter for now\n    if (!applyEvapPreset(preset)) {\n      throw new Error('Unknown paper-wetness preset: ' + preset +\n        '. Valid: ' + Object.keys(EVAP_PRESETS).join(', '));\n    }\n    return api;\n  },\n  paperWetnessPresets() { return Object.keys(EVAP_PRESETS); },",

Each is independent.\nconst tiles = [\n  { el: '#tile-a', svg: '/sketches/proj-a.svg', url: '/projects/a' },\n  { el: '#tile-b', svg: '/sketches/proj-b.svg', url: '/projects/b' },\n  { el: '#tile-c', svg: '/sketches/proj-c.svg', url: '/projects/c' },\n  { el: '#tile-d', svg: '/sketches/proj-d.svg', url: '/projects/d' },\n];\ntiles.forEach(t =&gt; mountThumbnail(document.querySelector(t.el), t.svg, t.url));</pre>\n        <p>\n          The hero canvas in the same page can run its own animation\n          via a fifth instance &mdash; e.g.\n          <code>heroWc.setAnimation('rainy')</code> &mdash; with no\n          coordination needed.       "description": "Insert v0.30 changelog entry",
      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.30 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-30\">v0.30 &mdash; Sketch mode + obliterate</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Sketch mode.</strong> New <code>sketchMode(opts?)</code> method bundles the settings needed for crisp fine-line drawings &mdash; like a fine-tipped felt pen on dry paper. Internally applies <code>brushSize(7)</code>, <code>paintLoad(1.6)</code>, <code>waterLoad(0.25)</code>, <code>pressure(0.95)</code>, <code>paperWetness('boneDry')</code>, <code>continuousFlow(false)</code>, <code>edgeDarkening(false)</code>, <code>fadePainting(false)</code>. All defaults overridable via the opts argument. Pure setter \u2014 chains: <code>wc.sketchMode().traceSVG(svgText)</code>.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Obliterate.</strong> New <code>obliterate(opts?)</code> method that destroys the current drawing via a wet wash. Two modes: <code>'water'</code> (splash-style rinse \u2014 lifts deposited pigment back into suspension, swirls it outward) and <code>'pigment'</code> (opaque wash of a chosen color covers the drawing). Returns a Promise that resolves after <code>durationMs</code> (default 500). Intended for thumbnail \u2192 click \u2192 transition UI patterns where you want to visually \"destroy\" a sketch before navigating away. Accepts <code>x</code>/<code>y</code> grid coords to center the wash where the user clicked.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Intensity scale.</strong> The obliterate function maps an <code>intensity: 'gentle' | 'normal' | 'extreme'</code> option onto the existing splash presets (<code>fineSpritz</code>, <code>default</code>, <code>bigSplash</code>) and onto deposit strength multipliers in pigment mode. Same physics under the hood &mdash; just a named lever for the common cases.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Thumbnail recipe.</strong> Added a worked example in the Agents docs showing the full project-thumbnail pattern: mount 4 independent instances via the factory, apply <code>sketchMode()</code>, trace an SVG into each, attach a click handler that calls <code>await obliterate({ mode: 'water', durationMs: 500, x, y })</code> at the click position, then navigate. Each instance owns its own RAF loop and state arrays \u2014 no coordination needed between them. A fifth instance can simultaneously run a hero animation via <code>setAnimation()</code> with no interference.</li>\n\n          <li>Implementation notes: <code>sketchMode</code> just calls the underlying setters in sequence \u2014 no new physics, no separate mode flag, no state to restore. <code>obliterate</code> wraps <code>splash()</code> for water mode and a sequence of large <code>paintAt</code> calls plus a leading splash for pigment mode, then returns <code>setTimeout</code>-based Promise. Concurrency-safe across multiple instances and within a single instance (overlapping calls interleave correctly through the lib state).</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Docs navigation accordion was broken</strong> &mdash; click handlers + scroll spy + active-link logic were lost in the v0.27 extraction. Restored from v0.26.2: clicking section headers toggles their child lists; scrolling between docs sections auto-expands the visible one; the heading nearest the activation line gets the <code>.active</code> highlight in the sidebar.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Docs navigation was transparent and unbounded on mobile.</strong> Below 760px, the nav was <code>position: static</code> with no background \u2014 it became a full-height block of links at the top of the docs section that users had to scroll through before reaching content. Now: capped at 40vh, visible panel surface with rounded border and backdrop-blur, internally scrollable. Reads as a distinct widget alongside the controls panel and perf overlay.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.29 ===== -->",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.30 shipped.** Two new high-level methods, both built from existing primitives as you asked.\n\n**`sketchMode(opts?)` \u2014 felt-tip pen settings bundle.** A pure setter that calls eight underlying API methods in sequence:\n\n```js\nbrushSize(7)              // fine tip\npaintLoad(1.6)            // denser pigment for line opacity\nwaterLoad(0.25)           // minimal moisture\npressure(0.95)            // firm strokes\npaperWetness('boneDry')   // no bleed\ncontinuousFlow(false)     // pure stamps\nedgeDarkening(false)      // clean lines\nfadePainting(false)       // strokes persist\n```\n\nNo new physics, no mode flag, no state-to-restore. Each default is overridable: `wc.sketchMode({ brushSize: 12 })`. Chains: `wc.sketchMode().traceSVG(svg)`.\n\n**`obliterate(opts?)` \u2014 the click-transition destroyer.** Returns a Promise that resolves after `durationMs` (default 500). Two modes:\n\n- `mode: 'water'` \u2014 calls `splash([{x, y}], preset)` which lifts all deposited pigment back into suspension and adds radial velocity. Pigment swirls outward as it diffuses. Best for \"rinse\" feel.\n- `mode: 'pigment'` \u2014 a leading splash to wet the paper, then five overlapping large `paintAt` calls in the chosen color. Opaque wash covers the drawing. Best for \"cover up\" feel. Pigment accepts a name (`'rose'`, `'cerulean'`) or numeric index.\n\nThe `intensity: 'gentle' | 'normal' | 'extreme'` option maps onto the existing splash presets (`fineSpritz` / `default` / `bigSplash`) and onto deposit strength multipliers in pigment mode \u2014 same physics, named lever for the common cases.\n\n**Project thumbnail recipe added to Agents docs** showing the full pattern: factory.create() per tile with `pointer: false`, sketchMode + traceSVG to draw, click handler that calls `wc.toGrid(clientX, clientY)` to convert click position to grid coords, then `await wc.obliterate({ mode: 'water', durationMs: 500, x: gx, y: gy })`, then navigate. Four tiles + a fifth instance for the hero animation, all independent, no coordination needed.\n\n**The V2 raster path will compose cleanly:** `obliterate` only cares about deposited pigment, not how it got there. A future raster-trace path that calls `paintAt` per sampled pixel produces the same wet/pigment state, so obliterate works unchanged.\n\n**Verified all paths** in the stubbed test: sketchMode applies all settings + accepts overrides, obliterate returns Promises that resolve on time, both modes work, pigment names + indices both resolved correctly, bad mode throws `Watercolor.obliterate: mode must be 'water' or 'pigment'`, defaults all work (water, 500ms, center, normal intensity).\n\n**Also included v0.30 fixes for the prior round** (docs nav accordion + mobile transparency) \u2014 those changelog bullets are in the v0.30 entry since they shipped together. Type signatures in the Agents docs now include `sketchMode` and `obliterate` with their option interfaces.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

For thumbnails on page load.\n  //   durationMs: <ms>   \u2192 spread the trace over roughly that many ms\n  //                        at 60fps. Round up so the trace completes in\n    // about durationMs or slightly sooner \u2014 better to finish a hair\n    // early than overrun a click-timed animation.\n    const framesAvailable = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(opts.durationMs / (1000 / 60)));\n    ppf = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(totalPts / framesAvailable));\n  }\n\n  svgTraceState = {\n    points: downSampled,\n    idx: 0,\n    pigment: pigment,\n    brushR: brushR,\n    strength: strength,\n    pointsPerFrame: ppf,\n  };\n  markCanvasActive();\n\n  // For instant mode, run the trace synchronously here so the caller\n  // can immediately await/depend on the deposited state without waiting\n  // for an RAF tick.\n  if (opts.instant) {\n    while (svgTraceState && svgTraceState.idx < svgTraceState.points.length) {\n      svgTraceStep();\n    }\n  }\n\n  return totalPts;\n}\n\nfunction svgTraceStep() {\n  if (!svgTraceState) return;\n  const st = svgTraceState;\n  const limit = st.pointsPerFrame || SVG_POINTS_PER_FRAME;\n  for (let i = 0; i < limit && st.idx < st.points.length; i++, st.idx++) {\n    const pt = st.points[st.idx];\n    paintAt(pt.x, pt.y, st.brushR, st.pigment, st.strength);\n  }\n  if (st.idx >= st.points.length) svgTraceState = null;\n}",

      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.30 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-30\">v0.30 &mdash; Sketch mode + obliterate</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Sketch mode.</strong> New <code>sketchMode(opts?)</code> method bundles the settings needed for crisp fine-line drawings &mdash; like a fine-tipped felt pen on dry paper. Internally applies <code>brushSize(7)</code>, <code>paintLoad(1.6)</code>, <code>waterLoad(0.25)</code>, <code>pressure(0.95)</code>, <code>paperWetness('boneDry')</code>, <code>continuousFlow(false)</code>, <code>edgeDarkening(false)</code>, <code>fadePainting(false)</code>. All defaults overridable via the opts argument. Pure setter \u2014 chains: <code>wc.sketchMode().traceSVG(svgText)</code>.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>SVG trace speed control.</strong> Two new <code>traceSVG</code> options. <code>instant: true</code> paints every point synchronously before the call returns &mdash; the trace finishes inside the same frame, no animation visible. For thumbnails on page load this is the right default. <code>durationMs: &lt;number&gt;</code> spreads the trace over that many milliseconds at 60 fps by dividing total points across the available frames. Calling with neither preserves the original ~10-points-per-frame behavior. <code>instant</code> takes precedence over <code>durationMs</code> if both are set.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Obliterate.</strong> New <code>obliterate(opts?)</code> method that destroys the current drawing via a wet wash. Two modes: <code>'water'</code> (splash-style rinse \u2014 lifts deposited pigment back into suspension, swirls it outward) and <code>'pigment'</code> (opaque wash of a chosen color covers the drawing). Returns a Promise that resolves after <code>durationMs</code> (default 500). Intended for thumbnail \u2192 click \u2192 transition UI patterns where you want to visually \"destroy\" a sketch before navigating away. Accepts <code>x</code>/<code>y</code> grid coords to center the wash where the user clicked.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Obliterate button in the controls panel.</strong> Sits next to <strong>Fine spritz</strong> in the action-button row. Click = water mode (default). Shift-click = pigment mode (opaque wash of the currently-selected pigment).</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Intensity scale.</strong> The obliterate function maps an <code>intensity: 'gentle' | 'normal' | 'extreme'</code> option onto the existing splash presets (<code>fineSpritz</code>, <code>default</code>, <code>bigSplash</code>) and onto deposit strength multipliers in pigment mode. Same physics under the hood &mdash; just a named lever for the common cases.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Thumbnail recipe.</strong> Added a worked example in the Agents docs showing the full project-thumbnail pattern: mount 4 independent instances via the factory, apply <code>sketchMode()</code>, trace an SVG into each with <code>{ instant: true }</code>, attach a click handler that calls <code>await obliterate({ mode: 'water', durationMs: 500, x, y })</code> at the click position, then navigate. Each instance owns its own RAF loop and state arrays \u2014 no coordination needed between them. A fifth instance can simultaneously run a hero animation via <code>setAnimation()</code> with no interference.</li>\n\n          <li>Implementation notes: <code>sketchMode</code> just calls the underlying setters in sequence \u2014 no new physics, no separate mode flag, no state to restore. <code>obliterate</code> wraps <code>splash()</code> for water mode and a sequence of large <code>paintAt</code> calls plus a leading splash for pigment mode, then returns <code>setTimeout</code>-based Promise. Concurrency-safe across multiple instances and within a single instance (overlapping calls interleave correctly through the lib state). SVG speed control stores <code>pointsPerFrame</code> on the trace state object so each trace honors its own pace.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Docs navigation accordion was broken</strong> &mdash; click handlers + scroll spy + active-link logic were lost in the v0.27 extraction. Restored from v0.26.2: clicking section headers toggles their child lists; scrolling between docs sections auto-expands the visible one; the heading nearest the activation line gets the <code>.active</code> highlight in the sidebar.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-fix\">fix</span> <strong>Docs navigation was transparent and unbounded on mobile.</strong> Below 760px, the nav was <code>position: static</code> with no background \u2014 it became a full-height block of links at the top of the docs section that users had to scroll through before reaching content. Now: capped at 40vh, visible panel surface with rounded border and backdrop-blur, internally scrollable. Reads as a distinct widget alongside the controls panel and perf overlay.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.29 ===== -->",

No animation \u2014 paint instantly during the call. For thumbnails on page load.\nwc.traceSVG(svg, { instant: true });\n\n// 2. Custom duration \u2014 spread the trace over N milliseconds at 60fps.\nwc.traceSVG(svg, { durationMs: 1500 });   // 1.5 seconds of drawing animation\n\n// 3. Default \u2014 original ~10 points/frame regardless of point count.\nwc.traceSVG(svg);\n```\n\n**Implementation:** the trace state now stores its own `pointsPerFrame` (was a module constant). On each frame the loop paints up to that many points and decrements. For `durationMs`, `pointsPerFrame = ceil(totalPoints / framesAvailable)` where `framesAvailable = ceil(durationMs / 16.67)`. For `instant: true`, the loop runs synchronously inside `traceSVG` before it returns \u2014 same code path, just executed all at once. `instant` takes precedence over `durationMs` if both are passed.\n\n**Verified in Node** (synthetic SVG with 101 sample points):\n- default \u2192 101 points queued, painted over ~10 frames\n- `instant: true` \u2192 101 points painted before return, completed in 32ms (mostly DOM-stub overhead)\n- `durationMs: 200` \u2192 ~9 points/frame\n- `durationMs: 50` \u2192 ~34 points/frame (much faster)\n- `sketchMode().traceSVG({instant:true})` chains correctly\n\n**Documentation updated** in three places:\n- The SVG API reference section (added `instant` and `durationMs` definitions to the deflist, updated the opts comment line)\n- The Agents type signatures (`SVGTraceOptions` interface now lists both)\n- The thumbnail recipe in Agents recipes (now uses `{ pigment: 'rose', instant: true }` with a comment explaining the choice and how to swap for a deliberate animation)\n\n**Changelog v0.30 entry** now has new bullets for the SVG speed control and the Obliterate button, plus the existing sketchMode + obliterate + thumbnail recipe + nav fixes.\n\nFor your project setup: thumbnails should use `instant: true` (drawn before any frame paints; user sees them already complete), and the hero animation in the other canvases can run independently at whatever pace you want via `setAnimation()` or programmatic loops.     "start_timestamp": "2026-05-12T02:40:39.608053Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-12T02:41:04.988693Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": " I need to tackle two things here: first, expanding the control panel to include animation parameters like duration and the existing drawing options, and second, thinking through what kind of timing function would feel natural for the trace animation\u2014whether that's a simple easing curve, a spring effect, or something that actually mimics the physical act of drawing.\n\nFor the timing, uniform speed across all frames feels mechanical. Ease-out would give that confident initial stroke that tapers off, which is how most drawing apps work. Ease-in-out adds care at both ends but might feel less natural. Spring overshooting doesn't make sense here since you can't undo a stroke. The real insight is that natural drawing has rhythm\u2014pauses where the pen lifts between segments, and variable speed based on how tight the curves are, so corners get more attention than straightaways. That's harder to compute but would feel the most authentic.\n\nFor a practical v0.31, I'd focus on the two easiest wins: adding easing options and pausing between separate SVG paths. The implementation would need to track which points belong to which path, then apply timing functions to control the drawing speed. I'm working through the math for how to distribute points across frames with easing applied. I'm realizing that variable speed based on curvature would feel most natural\u2014slowing down at corners like a real pen would\u2014but that's computationally expensive. A simpler approach is to vary the painting speed based on angle changes between segments. Though honestly, standard CSS easing functions like easeOut would already feel much more natural than linear, so let me start with that and track elapsed time rather than just counting points.\n\nSetting up the trace state... I'm realizing the per-stroke approach is getting too complicated, so I'll focus on shipping the simpler easing functions first\u2014linear, ease-out, and ease-in-out\u2014and keep per-stroke pauses as a separate toggle. This gives users real control over the animation feel without overcomplicating the initial implementation.       "description": "Refactor trace state for time-based easing, track path boundaries",
      "new_str": "  // v0.30 \u2014 Trace speed control. Three modes:\n  //   instant: true      \u2192 paint every point in one frame (synchronous,\n  //                        no animation). For thumbnails on page load.\n  //   durationMs: <ms>   \u2192 spread the trace over roughly that many ms.\n  //                        Uses the easing function for shape.\n  //   (neither)          \u2192 default ~10 points/frame, original behavior.\n  //\n  // v0.31 \u2014 easing. When durationMs is set, the trace honors an\n  // easing curve mapping elapsed-time-fraction to point-fraction. See\n  // _applyEasing below for the available curves.\n  const totalPts = downSampled.length;\n  let ppf = SVG_POINTS_PER_FRAME;\n  if (opts.instant) {\n    ppf = totalPts;\n  } else if (typeof opts.durationMs === 'number' && opts.durationMs > 0) {\n    const framesAvailable = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(opts.durationMs / (1000 / 60)));\n    ppf = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(totalPts / framesAvailable));\n  }\n\n  svgTraceState = {\n    points: downSampled,\n    pathStarts: pathStarts,\n    idx: 0,\n    pigment: pigment,\n    brushR: brushR,\n    strength: strength,\n    pointsPerFrame: ppf,\n    // v0.31 \u2014 time-based easing fields. When durationMs is set, the\n    // step function ignores pointsPerFrame and computes targetIdx\n    // from elapsed time + easing.\n    startTime: (typeof performance !== 'undefined' ? performance.now() : Date.now()),\n    durationMs: opts.durationMs,\n    easing: opts.easing || 'linear',\n    perStrokePauseMs: typeof opts.perStrokePauseMs === 'number'\n                      ? opts.perStrokePauseMs : 0,\n  };\n  markCanvasActive();\n\n  // For instant mode, run the trace synchronously here so the caller\n  // can immediately await/depend on the deposited state without waiting\n  // for an RAF tick.\n  if (opts.instant) {\n    while (svgTraceState && svgTraceState.idx < svgTraceState.points.length) {\n      svgTraceStep();\n    }\n  }\n\n  return totalPts;\n}\n\n// v0.31 \u2014 Easing curves. Map an \"elapsed fraction\" (0..1) to a \"drawn\n// fraction\" (0..1). All return 0 at 0 and 1 at 1. Names match common\n// CSS / animation libraries so values feel familiar.\nfunction _applyEasing(t, name) {\n  if (t <= 0) return 0;\n  if (t >= 1) return 1;\n  switch (name) {\n    case 'linear':     return t;\n    case 'easeIn':     return t * t;\n    case 'easeOut':    return 1 - (1 - t) * (1 - t);\n    case 'easeInOut':  return t < 0.5\n                              ? 2 * t * t\n                              : 1 - 2 * (1 - t) * (1 - t);\n    // 'penStroke' \u2014 meant to mimic a human drawing: a brief hesitation\n    // at the start (acceleration), steady drawing speed in the middle,\n    // brief slowdown at end of stroke. Pairs naturally with\n    // perStrokePauseMs > 0 for per-path pauses.\n    case 'penStroke':  return t < 0.15 ? (t / 0.15) * 0.10\n                              : t > 0.85 ? 0.90 + ((t - 0.85) / 0.15) * 0.10\n                              : 0.10 + ((t - 0.15) / 0.70) * 0.80;\n    default:           return t;   // unknown name \u2192 linear\n  }\n}\n\nfunction svgTraceStep() {\n  if (!svgTraceState) return;\n  const st = svgTraceState;\n  const total = st.points.length;\n\n  // v0.31 \u2014 time-based path when durationMs is set. Otherwise fall\n  // through to the original per-frame chunk behavior.\n  if (st.durationMs && st.durationMs > 0) {\n    const now = (typeof performance !== 'undefined' ? performance.now() : Date.now());\n    const elapsed = now - st.startTime;\n\n    // Per-stroke pauses: subtract total pause-time-already-spent from\n    // the effective elapsed timer so the easing curve maps onto active\n    // drawing time only. pauseDebt is the cumulative pause time that\n    // should have happened by the user's current point progress.\n    let pauseDebt = 0;\n    if (st.perStrokePauseMs > 0 && st.pathStarts && st.pathStarts.length > 1) {\n      // Count how many path-boundaries we've crossed.\n      let crossings = 0;\n      for (let i = 1; i < st.pathStarts.length; i++) {\n        if (st.idx >= st.pathStarts[i]) crossings++;\n      }\n      pauseDebt = crossings * st.perStrokePauseMs;\n    }\n    const effElapsed = Math.max(0, elapsed - pauseDebt);\n    const t = Math.min(1, effElapsed / st.durationMs);\n    const easedT = _applyEasing(t, st.easing);\n    const targetIdx = Math.min(total, Math.floor(easedT * total));\n\n    // Paint from current idx forward to the target. Cap per-frame at\n    // a reasonable upper bound so a long pause-then-resume doesn't\n    // dump hundreds of points in one frame and spike CPU; the next\n    // frame catches up if needed.\n    const cap = Math.max(SVG_POINTS_PER_FRAME, Math.ceil(total / 20));\n    let painted = 0;\n    while (st.idx < targetIdx && painted < cap) {\n      // Check if we just crossed a stroke boundary \u2014 if pauses are\n      // enabled, pause here until pauseDebt allows resuming.\n      if (st.perStrokePauseMs > 0 && st.pathStarts && st.idx > 0) {\n        const justCrossed = st.pathStarts.indexOf(st.idx) > 0;\n        if (justCrossed && elapsed < pauseDebt + st.perStrokePauseMs) {\n          break;\n        }\n      }\n      const pt = st.points[st.idx];\n      paintAt(pt.x, pt.y, st.brushR, st.pigment, st.strength);\n      st.idx++;\n      painted++;\n    }\n    if (t >= 1 && st.idx >= total) svgTraceState = null;\n    return;\n  }\n\n  // Original behavior \u2014 fixed points per frame.\n  const limit = st.pointsPerFrame || SVG_POINTS_PER_FRAME;\n  for (let i = 0; i < limit && st.idx < total; i++, st.idx++) {\n    const pt = st.points[st.idx];\n    paintAt(pt.x, pt.y, st.brushR, st.pigment, st.strength);\n  }\n  if (st.idx >= total) svgTraceState = null;\n}",
      "old_str": "  // v0.30 \u2014 Trace speed control. Three modes:\n  //   instant: true      \u2192 paint every point in one frame (synchronous,\n  //                        no animation). For thumbnails on page load.\n  //   durationMs: <ms>   \u2192 spread the trace over roughly that many ms\n  //                        at 60fps. Convert: pointsPerFrame =\n  //                        ceil(totalPoints * 1000 / (durationMs * 60)).\n  //   (neither)          \u2192 default ~10 points/frame, original behavior.\n  const totalPts = downSampled.length;\n  let ppf = SVG_POINTS_PER_FRAME;\n  if (opts.instant) {\n    ppf = totalPts;   // paint everything this frame\n  } else if (typeof opts.durationMs === 'number' && opts.durationMs > 0) {\n    // Frames available at 60fps. Round up so the trace completes in\n    // about durationMs or slightly sooner \u2014 better to finish a hair\n    // early than overrun a click-timed animation.\n    const framesAvailable = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(opts.durationMs / (1000 / 60)));\n    ppf = Math.max(1, Math.ceil(totalPts / framesAvailable));\n  }\n\n  svgTraceState = {\n    points: downSampled,\n    idx: 0,\n    pigment: pigment,\n    brushR: brushR,\n    strength: strength,\n    pointsPerFrame: ppf,\n  };\n  markCanvasActive();\n\n  // For instant mode, run the trace synchronously here so the caller\n  // can immediately await/depend on the deposited state without waiting\n  // for an RAF tick.\n  if (opts.instant) {\n    while (svgTraceState && svgTraceState.idx < svgTraceState.points.length) {\n      svgTraceStep();\n    }\n  }\n\n  return totalPts;\n}\n\nfunction svgTraceStep() {\n  if (!svgTraceState) return;\n  const st = svgTraceState;\n  const limit = st.pointsPerFrame || SVG_POINTS_PER_FRAME;\n  for (let i = 0; i < limit && st.idx < st.points.length; i++, st.idx++) {\n    const pt = st.points[st.idx];\n    paintAt(pt.x, pt.y, st.brushR, st.pigment, st.strength);\n  }\n  if (st.idx >= st.points.length) svgTraceState = null;\n}",

Default: <code>pigment()</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>brushSize</code></dt>\n          <dd>Brush diameter in display pixels. Default: <code>brushSize()</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>strength</code></dt>\n          <dd>Stamp strength, 0..1. Default: <code>pressure()</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>flipY</code></dt>\n          <dd>Mirror Y around the SVG's vertical center. Default <code>true</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>instant</code></dt>\n          <dd>When <code>true</code>, paints every point synchronously before <code>traceSVG</code> returns. No animation. Useful for thumbnails on page load.</dd>\n          <dt><code>durationMs</code></dt>\n          <dd>Spreads the trace over roughly this many milliseconds. The library tracks elapsed time per frame and uses <code>easing</code> to shape the rate.</dd>\n          <dt><code>easing</code></dt>\n          <dd>Speed curve. See the easing reference below. Default <code>'linear'</code> from the API; the demo panel defaults to <code>'penStroke'</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>perStrokePauseMs</code></dt>\n          <dd>Pause in milliseconds between SVG path elements. With multi-path SVGs, each path completes, then a brief pause, then the next starts &mdash; like a pen being lifted and repositioned. Default <code>0</code> (continuous).</dd>\n        </dl>\n\n        <h3>Easing curves for drawing animation</h3>\n        <p>\n          The trace animation needs a timing function &mdash; how\n          quickly to march through the point list as wall-clock time\n          progresses. Linear paints at a constant rate; eased curves\n          slow at certain moments to feel more natural. Choose based\n          on what feeling you want:\n        </p>\n        <dl class=\"docs-deflist\">\n          <dt><code>'linear'</code></dt>\n          <dd>Uniform rate throughout. Predictable; reads as mechanical or technical. Useful for procedural / CAD-style traces where the rhythm itself doesn't matter.</dd>\n          <dt><code>'easeIn'</code></dt>\n          <dd>Slow start, accelerating finish. Rare in drawing &mdash; feels hesitant at the start in a way that real strokes usually aren't. Mostly here for completeness.</dd>\n          <dt><code>'easeOut'</code></dt>\n          <dd>Fast start, decelerating finish. Mirrors a confident initial stroke that eases into the final position. Reads as casual but assured.</dd>\n          <dt><code>'easeInOut'</code></dt>\n          <dd>Slow at both ends, fast in the middle. Reads as deliberate &mdash; like the artist takes their time positioning at the start and finishing at the end. Good for \"careful drawing\" feel.</dd>\n          <dt><code>'penStroke'</code></dt>\n          <dd>Quick acceleration (~15% of duration), steady middle (~70%), brief deceleration at end (~15%). Closest to how a hand actually moves a pen through a continuous stroke. Pairs especially well with <code>perStrokePauseMs &gt; 0</code> for a believable multi-stroke drawing.</dd>\n        </dl>\n        <p>\n          For SVGs with many paths (logos, illustrations, line\n          drawings), <code>'penStroke'</code> with\n          <code>perStrokePauseMs: 100..300</code> mimics the actual\n          rhythm of a human sketching: smooth-ish strokes punctuated by\n          pen-lift moments. For single-path traces (signatures, single\n          curves), <code>'easeOut'</code> or <code>'penStroke'</code>\n          alone is fine.\n        </p>",

Fixes the common upside-down result from Y-up SVG sources (CAD exports, scientific plots, files originally drawn in Cartesian convention). Pass <code>false</code> if your SVG is in standard Y-down convention.</dd>\n          <dt><code>instant</code></dt>\n          <dd>When <code>true</code>, paints every point synchronously before <code>traceSVG</code> returns. No animation. Useful for thumbnails on page load where the trace shouldn't be visible. Default <code>false</code>.</dd>\n          <dt><code>durationMs</code></dt>\n          <dd>Spreads the trace over roughly this many milliseconds at 60 fps. Internally divides total points by <code>durationMs / 16.67</code> to compute points-per-frame. Default behavior (when neither <code>instant</code> nor <code>durationMs</code> is set) is ~10 points per frame regardless of total point count. <code>durationMs</code> overrides this; <code>instant: true</code> overrides both.</dd>\n        </dl>",

      "new_str": "        <!-- ===== v0.31 ===== -->\n        <h2 id=\"cl-0-31\">v0.31 &mdash; SVG trace easing + panel controls</h2>\n        <ul class=\"docs-list\">\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Easing curves for SVG trace animation.</strong> The trace step is now time-based when <code>durationMs</code> is set &mdash; it tracks elapsed wall-clock time, computes a target point index via an easing curve, and paints up to that point each frame. Five curves available: <code>'linear'</code> (uniform), <code>'easeIn'</code> (slow start), <code>'easeOut'</code> (fast start, slows to finish &mdash; reads as confident), <code>'easeInOut'</code> (slow at both ends &mdash; reads as deliberate), <code>'penStroke'</code> (15% ramp-up + 70% steady middle + 15% ramp-down &mdash; mimics how a hand actually moves a pen). Default <code>'linear'</code> in the API for backwards compatibility; the demo panel defaults to <code>'penStroke'</code>.</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>Per-path stroke pauses.</strong> New <code>perStrokePauseMs</code> option adds a pause between SVG path elements during the trace. The library tracks path boundaries through the downsample step, then pauses for the specified milliseconds when one path completes before starting the next. Pairs with <code>'penStroke'</code> easing to produce the rhythm of an actual drawing: smooth strokes punctuated by pen-lift moments. Default <code>0</code> (continuous trace).</li>\n\n          <li><span class=\"cl-tag cl-feature\">feature</span> <strong>SVG controls in the panel.</strong> The control panel now exposes the trace settings as live controls: a duration slider (0..8000ms, where 0 = instant), an easing dropdown (the five curves above), and a per-path-pause slider (0..800ms). Settings are read at trace time, so adjustments take effect for the next Load.</li>\n\n          <li>Implementation notes: the trace step has two code paths now &mdash; the original \"N points per frame\" mode when neither <code>durationMs</code> nor <code>instant</code> is set, and a new time-based path when <code>durationMs</code> is set. Time-based mode reads <code>performance.now()</code> at the start of each frame, subtracts accumulated pause-debt from per-path pauses, applies the easing curve to get a target point index, then paints from the current index forward. Caps per-frame work at <code>max(SVG_POINTS_PER_FRAME, ceil(total / 20))</code> to keep frame times bounded even after a long pause-then-resume.</li>\n        </ul>\n\n        <!-- ===== v0.30 ===== -->",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "**v0.31 shipped.** Three new SVG controls in the panel, time-based animation with five easing curves, and per-path pauses.\n\n## Easing \u2014 what to use when\n\nBefore the code: my actual recommendation for the timing function, since you asked.\n\n**Spring isn't a fit** for drawing. Springs overshoot and oscillate; you'd never see a pen *un-draw and re-draw* a stroke. Springs work for UI elements landing into position because the position itself is the goal \u2014 but a drawing isn't a position, it's a process. So spring's out.\n\n**Ease-out** is the safest default if you want one curve and you're done. It mirrors how confident strokes actually move: the pen accelerates quickly off the previous point, glides through the middle, decelerates as it approaches the next anchor. It's how most drawing apps animate playback. Reads as casual + assured.\n\n**Ease-in-out** is what to use when the drawing should feel *deliberate*. The slow-down at both ends reads as \"the artist is taking their time\" \u2014 start carefully, end carefully. Good for signatures, calligraphy, anything where the rhythm itself is meant to convey care.\n\n**Linear** is for cases where the rhythm should *not* convey anything \u2014 procedural CAD, technical diagrams, machine-generated graphics. The mechanical feel is the point.\n\n**The most natural option I implemented is `'penStroke'`** \u2014 a hybrid: a quick 15% ramp-up to get up to speed, then a steady 70% middle at consistent rate (this is when the pen is in its actual drawing motion), then a 15% deceleration to land. It's not based on any standard easing library \u2014 it's tuned specifically for how a hand actually moves a pen through one continuous stroke. The middle being linear (not eased) is what gives it that steady-hand feel; the ramps at the ends keep it from feeling robotic.\n\n**For multi-path SVGs, `penStroke` + `perStrokePauseMs: 100..300` is the magic combination.** Each path animates with the penStroke curve, then a brief pause (pen lifts, hand moves to next start position), then the next path. That rhythm \u2014 *stroke, pause, stroke, pause* \u2014 is what makes the trace read as \"a person drawing\" rather than \"an animation playing back\". For a typical logo or icon with 3-8 paths, try `durationMs: 2500, easing: 'penStroke', perStrokePauseMs: 200` as a starting point.\n\nThe demo panel defaults to `penStroke` + `150ms` pause precisely because it produces the most \"drawing-like\" feel out of the box.\n\n## Implementation summary\n\n**Lib changes:**\n\n- `_applyEasing(t, name)` maps elapsed-time-fraction (0..1) to drawn-point-fraction (0..1). Returns 0 at 0, 1 at 1 for every curve. `penStroke` is a 3-piece linear function: 0\u21920.10 in [0, 0.15], 0.10\u21920.90 in [0.15, 0.85], 0.90\u21921.0 in [0.85, 1].\n- `loadSVGAndTrace` now tracks path boundaries through both collection (`pathStartsRaw`) and downsample (`pathStarts`) so per-stroke pauses land on real boundaries even after distance-based downsampling.\n- `svgTraceStep` has two paths now: time-based when `durationMs` is set (reads `performance.now()` per frame, subtracts accumulated pause-debt, applies easing, paints up to the target index with a per-frame cap to keep frame time bounded), and the original \"N points per frame\" path for backwards compatibility.\n\n**Panel controls (3 new rows in the \"Trace SVG\" area):**\n\n- **SVG duration** slider, 0\u20138000ms, step 100. Set to 0 \u2192 uses `instant: true`. Otherwise \u2192 `durationMs: <value>`.\n- **SVG easing** dropdown \u2014 five options, defaults to `penStroke`.\n- **SVG path pause** slider, 0\u2013800ms, step 50, defaults to 150ms.\n\nEach slider has a live readout (the duration label shows `instant` when at 0, otherwise the ms value). Settings are read at trace time via `currentSVGTraceOpts()` so adjustments take effect for the next Load without needing to re-load.\n\n**Verified in Node:** all five easing names accepted, multi-path SVG produces correctly-remapped `pathStarts`, perStrokePauseMs combines with all easings cleanly, `instant: true` still bypasses everything for the synchronous case.\n\n**API docs** updated with: full deflist of the new options, dedicated \"Easing curves for drawing animation\" subsection explaining the trade-offs of each, recommended combinations (penStroke + perStrokePauseMs for multi-path). The Agents type signatures now include both `easing` (string union) and `perStrokePauseMs` (number).\n\n**Changelog v0.31 entry** documents both features plus the implementation note about the two-code-path step function and the per-frame cap.",

Visually equivalent to painting opaque\n  // paper-color paint over the area \u2014 except it dynamically follows\n  // whatever paperColor() returns (no separate \"white\" pigment to\n  // re-tune if you change paper color). Masked cells are frozen as\n  // usual \u2014 paper brush can't reach them.\n  if (pigmentIdx === PAPER_INDEX) {\n    const wetGain = 0.35 * waterLoadMult;  // mild wetness for visual feedback\n    for (let py = minY; py <= maxY; py++) {\n      for (let px = minX; px <= maxX; px++) {\n        const dx = px - gx, dy = py - gy;\n        const d2 = dx * dx + dy * dy;\n        if (d2 > r2) continue;\n        const i = py * GW + px;\n        if (maskActive && mask[i] > MASK_THRESHOLD) continue;\n        const falloff = 1 - Math.sqrt(d2) / r;\n        const f2 = falloff * falloff;\n        // Scale clearance by stamp strength + falloff. At full strength\n        // and stamp center, deposited pigment is wiped entirely; toward\n        // the edges of the brush footprint it tapers smoothly so brush\n        // strokes have soft edges rather than hard discs.\n        const clearAmount = strength * f2;\n        const keep = Math.max(0, 1 - clearAmount);\n        d[0][i] *= keep; d[1][i] *= keep; d[2][i] *= keep;\n        g[0][i] *= keep; g[1][i] *= keep; g[2][i] *= keep;\n        // Add a touch of wetness so the brush stroke \"carries\" \u2014 the\n        // wash bleeds outward through the simulation, blending into\n        // surrounding cells the way an opaque-paint stroke softens at\n        // its border on real wet paper.\n        const wetAdd = f2 * strength * wetGain;\n        const nw = wet[i] + wetAdd;\n        wet[i] = nw > 1 ? 1 : nw;\n      }\n    }\n    return;\n  }\n\n  // Rainbow brush \u2014 same dynamics as a regular pigment brush, but the",
